chunk_summary: Guidelines for feeding preterm and low birth weight infants focus on early trophic feeds, human milk fortification, feeding transitions, discharge plans, and monitoring growth using corrected age charts.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of early trophic feeding in preterm infants and its benefits.
Learn protocols for human milk fortification to meet nutrient needs of very preterm infants.
Recognize feeding transitions and developmental milestones for preterm infants.
Develop discharge plans including feeding schedules, supplementation, and growth monitoring.
key_facts: Minimal enteral feeds should start within 24–48 hours in stable preterm infants, advancing by 10–30 mL/kg/day.
Mother’s own milk is preferred as it reduces necrotizing enterocolitis risk; donor human milk is second-line for VLBW infants.
Human milk fortification is recommended once enteral volume reaches ~100 mL/kg/day to support growth and bone mineralization.
Feeding transitions occur as suck–swallow–breathe coordination develops, typically around 32–34 weeks gestation.
Discharge plans should include feeding schedules, fortified milk or postdischarge formula, vitamin D and iron supplementation, and growth monitoring using corrected age charts.
reference_quote: Mother’s own milk is first-line because it reduces necrotizing enterocolitis risk and is better tolerated; if unavailable, pasteurized donor human milk is preferred over formula for VLBW infants. (WHO Reference 1)
